content.text:
*"Consider how the wild flowers grow. They do not labor 
or spin."*{Luke 12:27}{A random variable is not a variable at all; it is a function from outcomes to numbers.}{Andrey Kolmogorov}

A **random variable** assigns a number to each 
outcome, allowing us to study numerical aspects of random phenomena.

content.title: Random Variable
content.content:
A **random variable** $X$ on sample space $S$ is a function 
$X: S \to \R$ that assigns a real number to each outcome.

content.title: Die roll
content.problem:
Roll a die. Let $X$ = the number showing. Then $X$ takes values in 
$\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$, each with probability $1/6$.

content.title: Coin flips
content.problem:
Flip a coin 3 times. Let $X$ = number of heads.

Sample space has 8 outcomes. $X$ takes values 0, 1, 2, 3 with probabilities:
\[ P(X = 0) = 1/8, \quad P(X = 1) = 3/8, \quad P(X = 2) = 3/8, \quad P(X = 3) = 1/8 \]

content.title: Probability Distribution
content.content:
The **probability distribution** of 
$X$ specifies $P(X = x)$ for each possible value $x$.

content.text: The probabilities must be non-negative and sum to 1.

content.title: Bernoulli Distribution
content.content:
A **Bernoulli trial** has two outcomes: 
success (probability $p$) and failure (probability $1 - p$).

If $X = 1$ for success and $X = 0$ for failure:
$P(X = 1) = p$, $P(X = 0) = 1 - p$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Binomial Distribution
content.content:
Let $X$ = number of successes in $n$ independent Bernoulli trials with 
success probability $p$. Then:
\[ P(X = k) = \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k} \]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Binomial
content.problem: A fair coin is flipped 10 times. What is $P(\text{exactly 6 heads})$?

content.title: Geometric Distribution
content.content:
Let $X$ = number of trials until first success. Then:
\[ P(X = k) = (1-p)^{k-1} p \quad \text{for } k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Geometric
content.problem: Roll a die until you get a 6. What is $P(\text{takes exactly 4 rolls})$?

marginNotes.text:
Can any one of you by worrying add a single 
hour to your life?
